merger_name: PT Asian Bulk Logistics – Engage Marine

other_parties.name: Australian Global Onshore Logistics Pty Ltd
AUSTRALIAN GLOBAL MARINE AND LOGISTICS PTY LTD

merger_description:
PT Asian Bulk Logistics, through its Australian subsidiary, Global Engage Marine Pty Ltd (**ABL**) proposes to acquire Engage Marine TopCo Pty Ltd (**Engage Marine**) (the **Acquisition**).

ABL operates three subsidiaries in Australia:

* Transhipment Services Australia, which provides bulk cargo transhipment services utilising floating barges to transfer materials, such as iron ore, directly between smaller ships or shallow terminals and larger ocean-going vessels in deepwater berths.
* One Rail Australia, a rail freight operator operating in NSW and central Queensland, specialising in transporting bulk commodities such as coal and minerals.
* SCF Containers, an Australian shipping container supplier offering a wide range of products for hire or sale, including shipping containers, site sheds, dangerous goods containers, tank and refrigerated containers, intermodal containers, trailers, and remote camps.

Engage Marine provides terminal and harbour towage including the berthing and sailing of ocean-going vessels at ports across Australia. Engage Marine also provides ad-hoc towage emergency response solutions in salvage situations.

Transhipment in this context refers to a logistics service concerned with getting a mined commodity from land to an ocean-going vessel.

events.title: PT Asian Bulk Logistics - Engage Marine - Straightforward Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Philip Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
PT Asian Bulk Logistics, through its Australian subsidiary, Global
Engage Marine Pty Ltd (ABL) proposes to acquire Engage Marine
TopCo Pty Ltd (Engage Marine) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, ABL operates three subsidiaries in Australia:
▪ Transhipment Services Australia, which provides bulk cargo
transhipment services utilising floating barges to transfer
materials, such as iron ore, directly between smaller ships or
shallow terminals and larger ocean-going vessels in
deepwater berths.
▪ One Rail Australia, a rail freight operator operating in NSW
and central Queensland, specialising in transporting bulk
commodities such as coal and minerals.
▪ SCF Containers, an Australian shipping container supplier
offering a wide range of products for hire or sale, including
shipping containers, site sheds, dangerous goods
containers, tank and refrigerated containers, intermodal
containers, trailers, and remote camps.
The target, Engage Marine, provides terminal and harbour towage
(towage services) including the berthing and sailing of ocean-going
vessels at ports across Australia. Engage Marine also provides ad-
hoc towage emergency response solutions in salvage situations.
Transhipment in this context refers to a logistics service concerned
with getting a mined commodity from land to an ocean-going vessel.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
The merger parties have a vertical relationship with Engage Marine’s
towage services being an input into the supply of transhipment
services in some circumstances.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third partes and publicly available
information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The merger parties are not close competitors in the supply of
transhipment or towage services.
• A vertical relationship between towage services and
transhipment services may arise at a port. However, the merged
firm is unlikely to have the ability and incentive to foreclose rival
transhipment service providers post-acquisition.
o This is because there are alternative options to Engage
Marine available to transhipment providers at those ports
where there is a current demand for transhipment services.
Those options include alternative towage service providers
currently operating at the port or the ability of some
transhipment customers to sponsor a new provider of
towage services to deliver the services they require at that
port.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.
